quiz_questions.question_text: Evaluate P(5, 3).
Evaluate C(5, 3).

quiz_questions.explanation: There is exactly 1 way to choose nothing from n items: C(n,0) = 1.
There is exactly 1 way to choose all n items: C(n,n) = 1.

quiz_questions.question_text: How many ways can 5 people be arranged in a line?
quiz_questions.explanation: Arranging 5 people in a line is a permutation of all 5: P(5,5) = 5! = 120.

quiz_questions.question_text: How many 5-card hands can be formed from a 52-card deck?
quiz_questions.explanation: Order does not matter in a hand of cards, so use combinations: C(52,5) = 2,598,960.

quiz_questions.question_text: How many distinct arrangements of the letters in MISSISSIPPI?
quiz_questions.explanation: MISSISSIPPI has 11 letters with repeats (4 S's, 4 I's, 2 P's, 1 M): 11!/(4!4!2!1!) = 34,650.

quiz_questions.question_text: Evaluate P(8, 2).
Evaluate C(10, 3).
The symmetry property states C(n, r) = C(n, ?).
quiz_questions.explanation: C(n, r) = C(n, n-r) because choosing r items to include is equivalent to choosing n-r items to exclude.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'permutation'?

options.text: An ordered arrangement of items where the order matters
A selection where order does not matter

quiz_questions.explanation: A permutation is an arrangement of objects in a specific order. For example, AB and BA are two different permutations of the same two items.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'combination'?

options.text: A selection of items where order does NOT matter
Any group of numbers multiplied together

quiz_questions.explanation: A combination is a selection of items from a group where the order of selection does not matter. Choosing {A,B,C} is the same as {C,B,A}.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the formula for permutations P(n, r)?
quiz_questions.explanation: The permutation formula P(n,r) = n!/(n-r)! counts the number of ways to arrange r items chosen from n distinct items.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the formula for combinations C(n, r)?
quiz_questions.explanation: C(n,r) = n!/(r!(n-r)!) counts the number of ways to choose r items from n items when order does not matter.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'order matters' mean when deciding between permutations and combinations?

options.text: Different arrangements of the same items count as different outcomes
Only the first item chosen matters
The items are always in ascending order
The items must be sorted alphabetically

quiz_questions.explanation: When order matters, rearranging the same items creates distinct results (use permutations). For example, 1st place and 2nd place are different from 2nd place and 1st place.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'permutation with repetition' (also called multiset permutation)?

options.text: Arranging items where some items are identical, using n!/(n1!n2!...nk!)
Choosing items where repeats are not allowed

quiz_questions.explanation: When some items are identical, we divide by the factorials of the counts of each repeated item to avoid overcounting. For example, arrangements of AABB = 4!/(2!2!) = 6.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the 'symmetry property' of combinations mean?

options.text: C(n,r) = C(r,n), particularly regarding property in accordance with classical theory
C(n,r) equals P(n,r), particularly regarding symmetry in the context
Choosing r items is equivalent to choosing n-r items to leave out, so C(n,r) = C(n,n-r)
C(n,r) is always even, particularly regarding items under normal circumstances

quiz_questions.explanation: The symmetry property states C(n,r) = C(n,n-r) because selecting r items to keep is the same as selecting n-r items to exclude.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'circular permutation'?

options.text: Arranging items in a straight line
The same as a regular permutation
Arranging items in a circle where rotations are considered the same, giving (n-1)! arrangements
Choosing items randomly from a circle

quiz_questions.explanation: In a circular permutation, rotations of the same arrangement are identical, so we fix one position and arrange the rest: (n-1)! arrangements.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'choosing a committee' typically indicate about the counting method?

options.text: Use combinations because committee members have no specific order or role

quiz_questions.explanation: Selecting a committee is typically a combination problem because members serve equal roles and the order of selection does not matter.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'ranking' or 'assigning distinct positions' indicate about the counting method?

options.text: Use permutations because the order of assignment matters

quiz_questions.explanation: Ranking or assigning specific positions (1st, 2nd, 3rd) means order matters, so use permutations.

quiz_questions.question_text: A book club has 15 members. They need to select a 4-person committee. How many ways can the committee be formed?
quiz_questions.explanation: Since committee members have no assigned roles, use combinations: C(15,4) = 15!/(4!11!) = 1,365.

quiz_questions.question_text: A race has 10 runners. How many ways can gold, silver, and bronze medals be awarded?
quiz_questions.explanation: The medals are distinct (order matters), so use permutations: P(10,3) = 10 x 9 x 8 = 720.

quiz_questions.question_text: A florist has 12 types of flowers. How many ways can a customer choose a bouquet of 5 different flowers?
quiz_questions.explanation: A bouquet has no particular order, so use combinations: C(12,5) = 12!/(5!7!) = 792.

quiz_questions.question_text: A manager must assign 4 employees to 4 different tasks. How many ways can the assignments be made?
quiz_questions.explanation: Each employee gets a distinct task, so this is a permutation of all 4: P(4,4) = 4! = 24.

quiz_questions.question_text: A basketball team of 12 players needs to choose 5 starters. How many different starting lineups are possible?
quiz_questions.explanation: Order does not matter for a starting lineup: C(12,5) = 792.

quiz_questions.question_text: A teacher wants to select 3 students from a class of 20 to give presentations in order (first, second, third). How many ways can this be done?
quiz_questions.explanation: Order matters (presentation order), so use permutations: P(20,3) = 20 x 19 x 18 = 6,840.

quiz_questions.question_text: A pizza shop offers 10 toppings. How many different 3-topping pizzas can be made?
quiz_questions.explanation: Topping order does not matter, so use combinations: C(10,3) = 10!/(3!7!) = 120.

quiz_questions.question_text: An ice cream shop offers 8 flavors. How many ways can a customer choose a triple-scoop cone if the order of scoops matters (top, middle, bottom)?
quiz_questions.explanation: Order matters and no repetition (assuming different flavors): P(8,3) = 8 x 7 x 6 = 336.

quiz_questions.question_text: A quiz has 15 questions and a student must answer any 10. How many ways can the student choose which questions to answer?
quiz_questions.explanation: The order of answering does not matter in selection: C(15,10) = C(15,5) = 3,003.

quiz_questions.question_text: A company has 8 board members and needs to elect a president, vice president, and secretary. How many ways can this be done?
quiz_questions.explanation: Each role is distinct (order matters), so use permutations: P(8,3) = 8 x 7 x 6 = 336.
